content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 29, 2000


Ms. Anne Stausboll
Chief Deputy Treasurer
Office of the Treasurer
P.O. Box 942809
Sacramento, CA 94209-0001

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-201

Dear Ms. Stausboll:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of State Treasurer Philip
Angelides regarding his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 83114; In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Is the State Treasurer permitted, under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, to
sign state-issued bonds even though the action may have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on one or more of his economic interests?

CONCLUSION

The State Treasurer may sign state-issued bonds despite a potential impact on an
economic interest of his, because signing the state-issued bonds is a ministerial act. The Act's
conflict of interest provisions do not apply to ministerial actions.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of
the California Code of Regulations. Please note that Commission regulations are currently being amended and
therefore some of the rules described in this letter may change. You may follow the progress of the pending
amendments to the conflict of interest regulations on the Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov).

Our File No. A-00-201
Page 2

FACTS

You provide the following facts, in pertinent part:

"The State Treasurer has various statutory duties and
obligations in connection with the State's issuance of general
obligation and revenue bonds. One such duty is to sign all bonds
issued by the State....

"....

"The Treasurer respectfully asserts that the affixing of a
facsimile of his signature on State general obligation and revenue
bonds is legally required for the bonds to be issued and is merely
ministerial in light of the fact that, by the time the bond instrument
is signed, all discretionary approvals and decisions regarding the
bond issuance have already been made and the affixing of the
signature is a mere formality. For these reasons, the Treasurer
requests your concurrence, in writing, that the Treasurer is
permitted to sign State-issued bonds, even in the face of a conflict
of interest under the Political Reform Act, provided that the
Treasurer does not participate in any of the determinations or
decisions leading up to the signing of the bonds where he has a
financial interest."

ANALYSIS

Applicable Law

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Generally, in order to determine whether the prohibition in
section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides an eight-step approach that
a public official may use to determine whether the official has a conflict of interest. The eight
steps are:

1. Is the individual a "public official?"

2. Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

3. Does the public official have economic interests?

Our File No. A-00-201
Page 3

4. Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

5. Will the financial effect of the decision on the public official's economic interest be
material?

6. Will the material financial effect of the decision on the public official's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?

7. Will the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the public official be
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally?

8. Is the public official's participation legally required despite the conflict of interest?

In addition, as an "elected state officer," the State Treasurer is also subject to section
87102.8.² Section 87102.8 provides:

"(a) No elected state officer, as defined in subdivision
(f) of Section 14 of Article V of the California Constitution, shall
make or participate in the making of, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision before the agency
in which the elected state officer serves, where he or she knows or
has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest.

"(b) An elected state officer knows or has reason to
know that he or she has a financial interest in any action by, or a
decision before the agency in which he or she serves where either
of the following occur:

"(1) The action or decision will have a direct and
significant financial impact on a lobbyist employer from which the
officer has received any salary, wages, commissions, or similar
earned income within the preceding 12 months and the action or
decision will not have an impact on the public generally or a
significant segment of the public in a similar manner.

___________________________
² Section 82021 defines "elected state officer" as "any person who holds an elective state office ...." Section 82024
defines "elected state office" as "the office of Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Attorney General, Insurance
Commissioner, Controller, Secretary of State, Treasurer, Superintendent of Public Instruction, Member of the
Legislature, member elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement System, and
member of the State Board of Equalization."

Our File No. A-00-201
Page 4

"(2) The action or decision will have a direct and
significant financial impact on any person, distinguishable from its
impact on the public generally or a significant segment of the
public, from whom the officer has received any compensation
within the preceding 12 months for the purpose of appearing,
agreeing to appear, or taking any other action on behalf of that
person, before any local board or agency.

"(c) The definitions of 'public generally' and
'significant segment of the public' contained in Section 87102.6
shall apply to this section.

"(d) Notwithstanding Section 87102, the remedies
provided in Chapter 3 (commencing with Section 83100) shall
apply to violations of this section."

Legal Analysis

You have not provided any facts concerning the basis for any actual conflict of interest.
For purposes of this discussion, we assume that the State Treasurer is confronted with an action
that, if considered a governmental decision, will have a foreseeable and material financial effect
on one or more of his economic interests. You also stated that the State Treasurer will not
"participate in any of the determinations or decisions leading up to the signing of the bonds
where he has a financial interest." Thus, for purposes of this letter we further assume that your
question pertains only to "making" the decision to affix his signature to the bonds. To the extent
that these statements and these assumptions are not correct, this advice is not applicable. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.)

Your question, in part, concerns the exception from the definition of "making" a
governmental decision applicable to ministerial decisions. Specifically, you have asked whether the
Treasurer is making a governmental decision before his agency when the Treasurer affixes a
facsimile of his signature on State general obligation and revenue bonds (this is the second step
in the standard conflict-of-interest analysis). A public official "makes a governmental decision"
when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency.³ (Reg. 18702.1.)

³ Section 87102.8 does not define "make" or "participate" or "using his or her official position to influence."
However, Commission regulations do define these terms for purposes of the conflict-of-interest rules of section
87100. Regulation 18702 provides: "To determine if a public official is making, participating in making, or using
or attempting to use his/her official position to influence a government decision, apply Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, sections 18702.1 through 18702.4, respectively."

Our File No. A-00-201
Page 5

Regulation 18702.4(a)(1) provides that making a governmental decision shall not include actions of public officials that are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. Ministerial actions do not constitute the making or participating in making of a governmental decision because they do not involve any discretion on the part of the official. (See Hahn Advice Letter, No. I-91-037.)

You state: "by the time the bond instrument is signed, all discretionary approvals and decisions regarding the bond issuance have already been made and the affixing of the signature is a mere formality." So long as the State Treasurer has no discretion regarding the signing of the bond issuance, the act is considered ministerial. Since the signing of these instruments is ministerial, the State Treasurer may sign the documents despite potential impacts on his economic interests.⁴

If you have any other questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

_______________
⁴ We have not gone on to discuss "legally required participation" since the conduct, based on your facts, is ministerial.

sign state-issued bonds even though the action may have a foreseeable and material financial

CONCLUSION: The State Treasurer may sign state-issued bonds despite a potential impact on an
economic interest of his, because signing the state-issued bonds is a ministerial act. The Act's
conflict of interest provisions do not apply to ministerial actions.
Please note that Commission regulations are currently being amended and
therefore some of the rules described in this letter may change. You may follow the progress of the pending
amendments to the conflict of interest regulations on the Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov).
